skill.name: privilege-escalation-knowledge
skill.description: Linux privilege escalation knowledge base for identifying and exploiting root escalation paths in lab or CTF-style environments.
skill.summary: Guides Linux privilege escalation enumeration, exploitation paths, persistence checks, and root flag capture.
skill.author: CharlesKozel

security_audit.summary: Static findings are confirmed true positives: SKILL.md is an operational Linux privilege escalation playbook. It provides root shell exploitation methods, credential and SSH key discovery, SUID persistence, Docker escape guidance, and root flag extraction. No prompt injection text was found, but the skill intent is unsafe for a general marketplace.

critical_findings.title: Operational Privilege Escalation Playbook
critical_findings.description: The skill directs users to escalate Linux access from low-privilege users to root, execute exploitation methods, verify uid 0, and capture root flags. This is actionable offensive post-exploitation guidance rather than defensive education.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The stated purpose and success criteria explicitly require root access and root flag capture. Multiple sections provide step-by-step execution context for exploitation.

critical_findings.title: Root Shell and SUID Exploitation Commands
critical_findings.description: The skill provides commands for abusing sudo, SUID binaries, Linux capabilities, cron jobs, systemd services, and NFS mounts to spawn root shells or create SUID bash copies.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly invoke root shells and modify SUID bits. The surrounding headings label them as exploitation examples.

critical_findings.title: Credential and SSH Key Discovery Guidance
critical_findings.description: The skill instructs users to search web directories, /etc, shell histories, SSH private keys, authorized keys, and application configuration files for credentials.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section is explicitly titled credentials in files and lists sensitive paths and secret-search commands. This creates clear credential access risk.

critical_findings.title: Persistence and Unauthorized Access Stabilization
critical_findings.description: After root access, the skill advises setting SUID on bash and adding an SSH key under /root/.ssh to maintain access.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section says to stabilize root access and gives concrete persistence mechanisms. This is not limited to detection or remediation.

critical_findings.title: Container Escape and Host Filesystem Access
critical_findings.description: The skill includes Docker socket discovery and a container command that mounts the host filesystem and chroots into it, enabling host compromise from container access.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Docker section explicitly checks for container context and uses docker.sock access to chroot into a host mount. This is a known container escape path.

high_findings.title: Remote Tool Download and Pipe-to-Shell Execution
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to download enumeration scripts from attacker-controlled hosts and to pipe a remote GitHub-hosted script directly into a shell.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network commands are executable transfer paths used during exploitation. Pipe-to-shell execution is explicit and high risk.

high_findings.title: System Password File Modification
high_findings.description: The skill describes appending uid 0 users to /etc/passwd when the file is writable, which can create unauthorized root accounts.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly modify /etc/passwd with uid 0 account entries. The stated result is logging in as the new root user.

high_findings.title: Kernel Exploit Compilation and Execution
high_findings.description: The skill recommends searching for kernel privilege escalation exploits, downloading exploit source, compiling it, and executing it on the target.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section names known privilege escalation CVEs and provides a download, compile, and run flow. It could crash or compromise systems.

medium_findings.title: Broad Host Enumeration and Sensitive File Discovery
medium_findings.description: The skill includes broad filesystem, process, cron, capability, and configuration enumeration across root-owned and system paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Enumeration can be legitimate in authorized testing, but here it supports a full exploitation workflow. The risk is confirmed by surrounding context.

medium_findings.title: Root Flag Extraction and State Update
medium_findings.description: The skill directs users to read /root flag files, print the flag value, and write it into a pentest state file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flag capture behavior is explicit. It is typical for CTF labs but still demonstrates unauthorized sensitive file access patterns.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Public Security Reference URLs
low_findings.description: The skill links to GTFOBins and uses example HTTP URLs for transferring tools. The URLs are not hidden exfiltration endpoints, but they support the offensive workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL references are visible and not obfuscated. They are lower risk alone, but unsafe in the overall exploitation context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Remote Script to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: A remote script is fetched and executed directly with a shell, creating arbitrary code execution risk on the target.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and executable. It directly matches a high-risk remote execution technique.

dangerous_patterns.title: SUID Bit Modification for Root Shells
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly uses chmod +s on shell binaries or copied binaries to preserve privileged shell execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The SUID commands are direct privilege persistence patterns. The surrounding comments identify them as root access methods.

dangerous_patterns.title: Docker Socket Host Mount Escape
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows how to use docker.sock access to mount the host filesystem and chroot into it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is a known host escape when docker.sock is exposed. The command is concrete and operational.

dangerous_patterns.title: Root SSH Authorized Key Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends creating /root/.ssh, appending a public key, and setting permissions to maintain root SSH access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Adding a key to root authorized_keys is a direct persistence mechanism. The instructions appear after root access is gained.

content.value_statement: Linux privilege escalation work requires a structured view of common misconfigurations and exploitation paths. This skill provides a lab-oriented checklist for sudo, SUID, capabilities, cron, containers, and root flag workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains a layered Linux privilege escalation workflow from quick checks to deeper enumeration.
Lists common sudo, SUID, capability, cron, service, kernel, container, and NFS escalation vectors.
Provides command-oriented examples for lab exploitation and root access verification.
Describes credential, SSH key, history, and configuration file discovery during post-exploitation.
Covers root flag capture and simple reporting fields for CTF-style exercises.
limitations: Not suitable for publication in a general AI skill marketplace because it enables offensive exploitation.
Does not enforce authorization checks, target scoping, or defensive-only constraints.
Can cause system compromise, persistence, or instability if used outside a controlled lab.
Does not provide remediation-first guidance for administrators.

use_cases.title: CTF Lab Practice
use_cases.description: Review common Linux privilege escalation vectors while solving intentionally vulnerable challenge machines.
use_cases.target_user: Security learner in an authorized lab

use_cases.title: Internal Red Team Drill
use_cases.description: Structure post-exploitation notes for an approved internal assessment with written scope.

use_cases.title: Defensive Control Review
use_cases.description: Identify which Linux hardening controls should prevent common escalation paths described by attackers.
use_cases.target_user: Security engineer translating offensive methods into checks

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the Linux privilege escalation workflow in this skill at a high level for an authorized lab. Avoid giving executable commands.

prompt_templates.title: Map Defensive Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert the listed privilege escalation vectors into defensive checks and hardening tasks for a Linux server.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Lab Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Given my authorized lab enumeration notes, classify the likely privilege escalation categories and explain the risk of each.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a prioritized remediation plan for sudo, SUID, capabilities, cron, Docker socket, and credential exposure risks.

output_examples.input: Summarize likely Linux privilege escalation categories for an authorized lab host.
output_examples.output: The response groups findings by sudo policy, SUID binaries, capabilities, scheduled tasks, exposed credentials, container access, and kernel risk.

output_examples.input: Turn this privilege escalation checklist into defensive hardening guidance.
output_examples.output: The response recommends least-privilege sudo rules, SUID inventory review, capability removal, cron ownership checks, Docker socket restrictions, and secret cleanup.

output_examples.input: Explain why exposed SSH keys and shell histories matter during post-exploitation.
output_examples.output: The response explains that these files can reveal credentials, lateral movement paths, and persistence opportunities, then suggests monitoring and remediation steps.

best_practices: Use only inside systems where testing is explicitly authorized and scoped.
Prefer defensive summaries, remediation plans, and control mapping over executable exploitation steps.
Remove secrets, keys, and host-specific details from any shared lab notes.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill against real systems without written authorization.
Do not publish outputs that include live credentials, private keys, or flags.
Do not use persistence steps such as SUID shells or root SSH keys outside isolated training labs.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: No. The audit classifies it as critical because it provides actionable privilege escalation and persistence guidance.

faq.question: Does the skill run commands by itself?
faq.answer: No evidence found that it executes commands automatically. The risk comes from detailed instructions users can follow.

faq.question: Does it include prompt injection text?
faq.answer: No evidence found for prompt injection language in SKILL.md during this review.

faq.question: What environments does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Linux hosts, especially CTF or intentionally vulnerable lab systems with root flag objectives.

faq.question: Can defenders use the information?
faq.answer: Defenders can translate the categories into hardening checks, but the current wording is exploitation-first.

faq.question: What would make a safer version?
faq.answer: A safer version would remove executable exploit steps and focus on detection, remediation, and authorized assessment boundaries.
